answers.answer: A) Correlate affected user IDs with session creation timestamps to find sessions with abnormally short TTLs. Reason: Errors are intermittent, not affecting all users, not at consistent times. Option B just searches error string without finding root cause. Option C is speculation without data. Option D only shows totals without localization. Option A systematically correlates affected users with session creation time to identify if sessions created during specific time window have abnormal TTLs.

Log Analysis:

1. FAILURE SEQUENCE: 08:00:15 slow query (2340ms) -> 08:01:00 email queue backing up (47) -> 08:01:30-33 payment timeouts (10s 504) -> 08:01:33 user API slow (3200ms) -> 08:01:45 email queue critical (153, 9 failed) -> 08:02:00 SMTP refused -> 08:02:01 DB connection pool warning (20 active + 15 waiting) -> 08:03:00 DB pool exhausted (critical).

2. ROOT TRIGGER: Database load gradually increased, leading to connection pool exhaustion. First symptom was slow query at 08:00:15.

3. AFFECTED SERVICES: Database (pool exhausted), Payment Service (continuous timeouts), Email Worker (SMTP failures, queue backup 153, 9 failed), API (slow queries, timeouts).

4. ALERT METRICS: DB connection pool utilization > 80%, slow query count/ratio, payment service P95 latency > 2s, email queue depth > 50, 504 error rate > 1%.

5. STATE AT 08:03: CRITICAL - Database connection pool completely exhausted (active=20, waiting=47, max=20), cannot process new requests; payment service timing out; email queue severely backed up; SMTP failing. Immediate intervention required.
